speaker: interviewer
text: OK, it's November the 8th, '94, survivor Hass Sigi, interviewer, David Cutler. Los Angeles, English, it's the fourth tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were going-- we were in the train and, at this moment, when I was in the train, I realized I was no more child. It-- it became to me like you take away a-- a curtain, that this is the real thing, that, here, something is going to happen. We were in the train. We came after eight days. We arrived in Birkenau. Of course, we didn't know at this time it was Birkenau. We arrived. They opened the-- the doors from the train. I saw a round gate and a platform with-- it looks to me like monkeys, a lot of the inmates, with striped pajamas and the Germans with the-- with their black boots and pens. [SPEAKING GERMAN] And right away, they separated women from men and-- and-- and pushed in a corner. And here were all the men standing, and SS men with a whip, with a horse whip, came and touched, touched men on the-- on the shoulder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, immediately, there was a inmate, pulled him away to another corner. So he went about 12 times, 12 people were pulled out from this transport, 12 men. And I was standing the last one. My dad, when he jumped, he left the coat, the long coat on with a shawl over my face. And the SS men asked me what is this. Is this a girl or a boy? I says, [SPEAKING GERMAN], means I am a man, not a female. So he touched with a whip and put me-- and I was the-- 13. And father of my friend Rudy Goldman, 14 people were chosen in one corner. And the rest were pushed together with the-- right away with the men and women, put out-- put back on trucks, and taken away. We were standing there while S men-- SS men came. He said to us, do you feel good? Does anybody feel sick or something? My friend's father says he doesn't feel too good. He had two surgeries. He says, OK, you go with the women and men. They went straight to the gas, what we found out. Straight from there, they went into the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we were left 13 men. We were chosen to go into the camp for work. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we came in. We came into a barrack. We had to undress completely, were shoven-- shoven hair, under arm, wherever there is-- we were shoven.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those people who did the hair cuts to us told us right away. You see, you see-- we saw the chimney. We saw through the window big chimneys with big-- big buildings with chimneys, and was a lot of smoke coming out. They says, you see, this is where your-- your your people are coming out now. We-- we thought as a joke, you know? He said-- I said, what do you mean? They went to the other barracks. No, they went to the gas chambers, you know? At this moment, everybody realized we were in Auschwitz, in the death camp. So when we were shoven, we were put in from one back to the other to go to a shower. We went to the shower. We came out-- we left out our clothes, our shoes and pants. Everything was left. We got into the next barrack, and we came out prisoners in-- in stripes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were put into a quarantine for four weeks, where we worked inside the camp. Then we did-- after this, we are put in-- a-- in-- this was Birkenau. We were put in the main D camp for-- or where they did all the labor. We were working there through the winter, where it was very, very, very hot. And in April, they were looking for transport to the Jawiszowice ghetto to clean up. They put together two transports. I was one of the transport to go. One transport was sent to Jawiszowice. The-- the other one was not sent. It was sent to Buna. Buna was a factory, a chemical [INAUDIBLE] farm factory where they used to make-- where they made rubber and tires, all chemical things for the war effort. In Buna, I worked for-- until January of 1945, when we heard already the Russian cannons in the background and the Germans prepared the camp to-- to evacuate. We were evacuated. We walked three days to Gleitwitz. In Gleiwitz, we already street fighting by the Russians, when they still had time to put us on wagons. And we were transported to Nordhausen. Nordhausen was a factory for V-2 rockets, where they shipped-- where they sent over to London. We were-- this factory was underground. It was in a mountain carved out from the rocks. We were working-- I was working there until the end of March 1945, when we heard already cannons again in the background. It was fighting around the city of Nordhausen. We were next to Nordhausen. And we-- the Germans had, again, time to evacuate us, put us on flat wagons, on trains. And we were, again, few days on the road. And we ended up in Bergen-Belsen. Bergen-Belsen, we did not work. We did not eat. We didn't do nothing. We did just starve. We were eating leaves. We were eating whatever came in the-- people were-- it's known that people were eating pieces of other people. The hunger was this big. One-- one day, one morning, we heard a very, very funny noise, like-- like a million of chains rattling. And-- and it came closer and closer. And the soldiers were gone. All of a sudden, there was no more guards. And here, the British unit-- [CRYING] British army liberated us. [CRYING] This was on April 15, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were free. We-- we were that free. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't know where to turn, no family, nobody. And after a few days, different commissions came from different countries. We had-- from all over the world, people from Greece, from France, from-- from Germany, Poland, all inmates. And, of course, the-- the people from France and Belgium took their people right away. They didn't want them to linger around in it camp there, in Bergen-Belsen. So I talked pretty well French. Papers we didn't have. I just said the number. And I went to the French commission and said, I am French-born on the border from [INAUDIBLE]. And my name-- I gave my real name. And I said, I am French citizen. I would like to go back home. So they considered me as a Frenchman, and I got a-- a paper showing that I'm French. And two days later, my name was called out. And all the French people were sent on trucks to-- to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before you go on, can I ask you some more questions about your camp experiences? Were you ever able to do anything while you were at the camps to get more food or-- or to get any special circumstances? Or how-- how did you survive those experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food we got was-- we got in the morning a-- a portion of bread, a little piece like this. And so-called tea or coffee, something black to drink. And at night, when we came back, we got a soup. And when you were at work, we got-- they bought out some soup too. Of course, it was not enough. It was just one-- one spoon of soup. And-- and-- what we got, and I had to-- I made some friends at the camp who were also from Berlin. And, you know, one tries to help the other one. And they let me wash the-- the-- what do you call it, where you put the food in, like thermos, big thermos. I could wash him, and there's always some food left, so I could gather some extra food and-- and have extra for me. And I helped some friends of mine by giving what I have too much. And I survived by washing some socks and pants for the kapos. The kapos was dressed better-- they were dressed better. They got more food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some days, when-- when we were in camp, we had to do camp work, so I was washing laundry for my kapo, and I got some extra bread for it. So-- Was this at Nordhausen or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was in Buna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Buna.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Birkenau, I was living straightly from the ration what we got. It was very meager. The Germans were very surprised that we could survive. They figured out that we can survive maximum three months with the food they're giving us. But we survived longer. And we were working outside. Sometimes, you found something. There were British and-- and-- British soldiers were also in these factories working. And they brought, sometimes, extra soup, and they put it in the corner so we can take it because we were not allowed to have any contact with any other people than with our own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anyone that you knew while you were at these three camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only one I knew was one man in my age, one boy my age, who came together with me. And I know only that, from the certain people, there's-- I am left, and the last one I knew was the-- the boy-- the boy who was with me, I lost contact in January 19-- in-- in Buna. But, as far as I know, I'm the only survivor. If this man lives, then we are two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any famous people that you-- you were with when you were in these camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a French Olympian swimmer, French Moroccan. At this time, it was like being French. His name was Nakache. He was-- he was in the camp. And there was a young boy, maybe a year or so older than I, who was from Greece. His name was Solomon. He was boxing in-- in Buna for making-- getting a little bit more bread. I know him very well. He is in Israel now. I know there came a movie out from him, but I never saw him back since I am from-- since I was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any time that you had do something in the camps that-- that you don't talk about or circumstances that made you do something that you wouldn't have normally done in those circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to-- had to-- to pick up dead bodies form bombardments. I mean, that's not usually work, but it was very common in the camp. When-- when somebody died, those people died on the-- on the march in, to make it harder for us to-- after 12 or 14 hours work at a camp-- at a-- at a work site, so to march in, we had to take two bricks and walk in, whole commandos with bricks, because the Germans wanted to build a new building. So they didn't want to spend gasoline for the trucks, so they made inmates-- we were 10,000 of them-- imagine, if 10,000 people bring every day two or three bricks in, so they-- they-- they saved the-- the transportation. This was a hard, hard thing because we walked about 40 minutes, half an hour, in the snow, with wooden shoes. In winter, oh, it was icy. You-- you were sliding. Or in the fall, it was melting. It was like going in-- in the mud ankle-deep. It was very hard. They-- they tried to make it as hard as possible for us. And instead of bringing-- let's say if they have to transport dirt, instead of taking it in little lorrys, we had to carry it in boxes with handles on, two people, and doing it while running.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they tried-- they tried everything. I-- like I said at the beginning, I was very strong. I worked all my life. And I was kind of conditioned. I was a big optimist. I always said I'm going to come out. From where my mother's born, I'm not going to die. And that helped me. When you look back over that period, is there any special incident or event that comes to mind that you want to talk about now before we go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] There's so many incidents that I don't feel that there's any special. Everything is unfortunately special for other people who didn't go through it. But it became a way of life and death. We tried to survive. And-- and people endure a lot of things to survive, even steal some other's bread, which was like death-- death sentence. You know. But-- and then if you go to work and you didn't work fast enough, you came home, you got 25 on your behind. You know. I get once five because I wanted to make some more soup in the evening, and, you know, we were 900, 800 people in one barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was-- for the toilet to go, you cannot go at night to the toilet. There was a big barrel. And men were peeing in there. And when it got full, somebody has to take it out at night or whenever. So everybody who had to go at night to the toilet, you know, was watching the barrel.

speaker: interviewee
text: If it is three quarter, do we rather make in the mattress than to go at night in the winter to carry out the-- two men were carrying about 100 liter. I mean, it's-- it's very heavy. To get out in the night, in the snow, or whatever, and-- and take it to the toilet and empty it. So I was night guard there. And it was up to me. When I saw the barrel is full, when somebody came to urinate, I had to keep him sitting there till the second one came to keep him to and to put him out for-- for-- to empty it. Once, I fell asleep. And my block-- the men that was responsible for the barrack caught me. I was just dosing. And, of course, I got right away got hit by him. He woke me up with two big-- I don't know. In French, you say [SPEAKING FRENCH]. [SPEAKING GERMAN] in German. He hit in the face.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I woke up. And he says, I want you in the morning to the appel. And I got five over my behind because I fell asleep during the-- my duty. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And another thing I remember very, very vividly, the first day I came to the camp, and we were tattooed. And I went to the barrack, and it was mostly Polish people. And the called the numbers for-- for food, they called in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know Polish. When my number was called, and I didn't answer, they had an extra portion of food. They were very glad that I didn't answer. But I made sure the next day that I know my number in Polish. And I can still remember [SPEAKING POLISH]. This is my-- my number that I had to remember. There was no name calling. You were just a number. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, of course, when we were liberated, I-- I didn't believe it. We had to go touch the soldiers. [CRYING] But it was true. And we were sent to France first by truck. Then they took us in an airplane, in military airplanes. It was, I remember till today, the Dakota 3, where the parachutist went out. They-- they had air fleet in [? Rymes, ?] not far from Belgium, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us in the airplanes. They took us through Belgium, Brussels, marched us through the main street in our uniforms. I changed my uniform in Bergen-Belsen. I found a-- a dead German. I took his jacket. And this was warm. It was in April, so it's still cold. And I arrived until Paris with a German uniform jacket. And in Paris, the committee, the Jewish Committee took care of me. And I want to just to-- to retrace my route from when we went over to Italy, maybe I find my parents. My mother was left in France when we left for Italy. She was in the camp. She couldn't get out from the camp. She was left for dead. She had typhoid fever. She was left in the camp. The Germans said she will die. We don't have to transport her. She was saved by French people. I found it out later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came to France, I wanted to trace back. We were going from France to Nice, and Nice to Saint Martin. I wanted to-- even was ready to walk my way back to Italy to trace. And coming in to-- to Nice, to the train station, was the Jewish Committee who welcomed-- we got passes to ride on trains, and buses and-- free, as [SPEAKING FRENCH], they called us. Not the Jewish deportees, [SPEAKING FRENCH]. So I had this pass. We had a special-- reserved the wagon to travel. I went to Nice. And in Nice, when I went out from the train, there was a Jewish Committee. And there was a girl I knew from the Nice at the time I was there. She saw me, and I saw her. [CRYING] We embraced. And she says, Sigi, from where are you coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all thought I'm dead. And she told me, at this instant, at the train, my mother's alive in Toulouse, what was a few hours' train away. And my father was saved in Italy. My brother, at this time, was in Palestine, was also saved. And my sister was in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first thing they did, telling my mother that I'm alive. And I went to see her. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] So a few days later, I arrived in-- in Toulouse, where she was and-- excuse me. The thing is arriving in Toulouse is fine, but-- but what they did, they-- Toulouse, instead of a taxi, they had a Red Cross wagon parked for me. And I arrived at 5:00 in the morning. And they kept me. I didn't want to wake my mother. I knew 5 o'clock in the morning to arrive, so there was this Red Cross wagon from the military. They thought they getting here a body, you know? They were waiting at the train station in Toulouse for me. My mother didn't know when I'm going to arrive, but I had the address already. So I came to Toulouse. They take me-- put me in the Red Cross wagon. I was sitting there. And they drove me to the house where my mother was living with people who saved her. And when-- when the car drove on to the house, my mother came. She was on the second floor looking out from the window. We were both looking and I-- you know, we couldn't talk, of course. And then I run up, and we embraced. And then I found out exactly. She did not know I was deported. My father wrote her letters through the Red Cross. And he always wrote about my brother [? Willy ?] and Mania, how nice everything is. She said what's with Sigi? Why don't you write from-- oh, Sigi's fine. He's working with the peasants.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, yeah, I want to have a picture from all four of you, my mother. So my father didn't know what to do. He took, you know, an old picture and he send it to her. And my mother recognized it, that it was not a picture from-- she knew that it was a picture from before. She had it. And as-- as-- as-- the-- as it happened, she-- she only knew from my deportation a few months before I came back that she find out that I was deported, and nobody knew where I was, until 1945, where I gave also my name to the Red Cross and announced that I'm alive and back from Auschwitz. If anybody's looking for me, that I am in-- [? in ?] [? fact ?] liberated from Bergen-Belsen. And, of course, the most thing, I wanted to-- to escape from Germany. I was so afraid that maybe they-- they will return. They will get strong again and-- and beat the Americans and catch Jews again. I was so afraid. I was walking in the street and turning around. I didn't believe that I was free to walk in a street where I wanted. I was asking my mother if I could eat. I was hiding bread under my pillow still in France for awhile. It took me a little time till I got used to normal life. I was sent from the French government. This what they did. They send me for four weeks on rehabilitation place to gain weight, to gain strength, and to become normal, I mean, to-- to live a normal life. They send me to a place called [? Lughda. ?] [? Lughda ?] is a very famous place in France from the-- from-- they have a saint, a saint girl, who-- I don't know their religion. That's about a saint in [? Lughda. ?] And it's a beautiful place near the mountains near the Pyrenees. And that's where I went for four weeks. I recuperated. I gained weight, and came back to my mother. And I told her that I am not going to stay in Europe. I want to go to Palestine at this time. It was Palestine. She said, it's very hard to-- to go to Palestine because you have to get-- it's called a certificate. The British gave a certificate only to certain people. They didn't give-- I said to my mom, I'm going to Paris, I'm going to the English Consulate. I was liberated by them. The least they can do is to unite me with my family. I will tell them that all my family is in Palestine so they have to send me. And that's what I did. In July of 1945, I arrived in-- in Haifa on a British warship called the I mean, it's not a war-- it's a transport, military transport ship, bought me from Marseille to Haifa in 1945, why I was the first transport. We were about 200 deported people back from the camps who went to Palestine. We were the first one to arrive in-- in Haifa. In Haifa, we were put in a camp again called [? Atlit. ?] [? Atlit, ?] here, we're coming to-- free-- to-- we're free. And they put us behind barbed wire again. Of course, we started to revolt and scream and not eating. And the British-- the British were guarding it. It was under British mandate at this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, well, you're here in a quarantine. You don't have to be afraid. You're free people. Just that we make sure you don't go out, maybe you bring a sickness from some place, you know? So we want you to make sure that you're all healthy, then you can go wherever you want. It was true. We were there for two-- two, three weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, of course, knew that I was there. He came to pick me up. And from there, we went to Tel Aviv, and I lived with my grandmother. My grandmother was living in Tel Aviv. She took us in, my brother and me. And my mother was still in France. And when I was on the transport to Palestine, my father was still in Italy. And when he heard that I go to Palestine, he took a taxi from Rome with-- with-- with a friend's father whose son was with me on the-- on the boat. He wanted to-- he came to Napoli by taxi to take us off the boat. He want us-- he did not want us to go to Palestine. So they-- they missed the boat-- like you said, they missed the boat. They saw the smoke, the boat leaving Napoli, when-- when they arrived there. So it was too late. We went to Palestine. And my-- my father decided that they will come too. They joined us and-- my father joined in 1945, later on. And my mother, too, joined. She came-- believe it or not, she came with the children transport. She was smuggled from-- with the children transport to Haifa from France. So she arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother, my father, my brother, and I, we were together, all together in Israel-- in Palestine. And my sister was in America. So she-- she went to America because she thought that everybody was killed, was-- she knew only we were deported. She didn't know that my brother and father escaped, and my mother was taken for dead anyway a long time ago. So the [INAUDIBLE] invited a boat of 900 refugees, a gesture in 1990-- in 1944, and my sister-- my sister was on this boat, was-- was taken to Fort Ontario. It was also a camp where they were supposed-- just to stay there over the war and then sent back after the war. But things changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I joined. I lived in-- in-- in Israel. I was already a little bit more freer. I joined the-- the Haganah in Israel. And it was the thing to do. We were young. We were a group. And we said we were going to fight. We never will let us-- nobody's going to take us life again, like we went, like, cheap to the-- to our deaths, you know, to the camps. Whenever somebody want something, they will have to fight for us. And since then, I-- I have kind of love for army, for-- for the Israeli army. I-- I have guns here in my house. I mean, I became a gun lover for-- for-- for just the sake of guns, target shooting, and things. I was excellent shot in the army. And lived in Israel until 1947. It started with the clearing. They wanted Medinat Yisrael. The And, of course, it became a 1948--
